coredata.dc:title: Engineering graphene/quantum dot interfaces for high performance electrochemical nanocomposites in detecting puerarin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hucheng
Shang, Yingying
Zhang, Ting
Zhuo, Kelei
Wang, Jianji
coredata.dc:description: CdTe quantum dots (QDs) capped by 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate ([emim]MP) and the reduced graphene oxide (RG) modified by the amine-terminated ionic liquid ([amim]Br) were synthesized in this work. The coordinating interface interactions between QD and RG render the nanocomposite the excellent electrochemical performances as revealed by cyclic voltammetry, chronocoulometry, and electrical impedance spectroscopy. Based on the experimental results obtained from amimRG, CdTe@MP-amimRG, CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG, and CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG nanocomposites, it is proposed that the synergistic effects between imidazolium rings and graphene are responsible for the enhanced performances. Taking the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG nanocomposite as the electrochemical sensor of puerarin, the proposed method demonstrates the linear range over 0.01–4.0μmolL−1 with the detection limits of 6nmolL−1 in the aqueous solution (S/N=3). Moreover, the sensor exhibits fast response time (2s), high sensitivity (74.57μAμmolL−1 cm−2), as well as reproducibility (RSD≤8%), and can maintain the stability at 4°C for two weeks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Ionic liquids
Quantum dots
Nanocomposite
Electrochemical sensors
Puerarin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of functional ligand-capped CdTe QDs
Preparations of nanocomposite and modified electrode
Electrochemical sensing for the detection of puerarin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21073055 and 21573059 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of as-synthesized CdTe@[emim]MP QDs, and the high-resolution TEM image of a single CdTe nanocrystal in the inset. (B) Histogram of CdTe@[emim]MP QD size distribution.
(A) FT-IR spectra; (B) Raman spectra of amimGO, and amimRG; (C) XRD patterns; (D) TGA curves. The corresponding materials: GO (a), amimGO (b), amimRG (c), and CdTe@[emim]MP- amimRG (d).

(A) Nyquist diagrams of electrodes in 2.5mmolL−1 [(Fe(CN)6]3−/[(Fe(CN)6]4− (1:1) solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl, and the equivalent circuit in inset; (B) CVs of electrodes in 10μmolL−1 puerarin aqueous solution containing H2SO4 (pH=1) at the scan rate of 100mVs−1, and Ipa for different electrodes in inset; (C) Dependence of Ipa on υ for CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE in 10μmolL−1 puerarin solution containing H2SO4 (pH=1), and CVs at different scan rates of 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300mVs−1 in inset; (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 50μmolL−1 puerarin aqueous solution and the corresponding spectra after the solution was respectively equilibrated with different electrode materials. Here, bare GCE (a), RG/GCE (b), amimRG/GCE (c), CdTe@MP-amimRG/GCE (d), CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG/GCE (e), and CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE (f).

Chronocoulometric curves of electrodes in 1.0mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl (A), and the linear regressions of Q vs. t1/2 respectively obtained from electrodes in 1.0mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl (B), in H2SO4 solution of pH=1 (C), and in 5mmolL−1 puerarin solution containing H2SO4 (pH=1) (D). The corresponding electrodes: bare GCE (a), RG/GCE (b), amimRG/GCE (c), CdTe@MP-amimRG/GCE (d), CdTe@[bmim]MP- amimRG/GCE (e), CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE (f).

Dependence of Ipa on c for CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE in H2SO4 aqueous solution of pH=1 (A), and in human plasma containing H2SO4 (pH=1) (B). Inset: DPVs with the puerarin concentration from (a) to (k): 4, 3, 2, 1.5, 1, 0.6, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1, 0.05, and 0.01μmolL−1, respectively.
Several parameters of the nanocomposite-modified GCE.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The physical adsorptive capacity of the nanocomposite was measured by UV–vis spectrum of puerarin aqueous solution.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative calibration parameters measured by DPV for the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE.
Determination of puerarin concentration in human plasma samples (n=5).

head.ce:title: Engineering graphene/quantum dot interfaces for high performance electrochemical nanocomposites in detecting puerarin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Centre of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CdTe quantum dots (QDs) capped by 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate ([emim]MP) and the reduced graphene oxide (RG) modified by the amine-terminated ionic liquid ([amim]Br) were synthesized in this work. The coordinating interface interactions between QD and RG render the nanocomposite the excellent electrochemical performances as revealed by cyclic voltammetry, chronocoulometry, and electrical impedance spectroscopy. Based on the experimental results obtained from amimRG, CdTe@MP-amimRG, CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG, and CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG nanocomposites, it is proposed that the synergistic effects between imidazolium rings and graphene are responsible for the enhanced performances. Taking the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG nanocomposite as the electrochemical sensor of puerarin, the proposed method demonstrates the linear range over 0.01–4.0μmolL−1 with the detection limits of 6nmolL−1 in the aqueous solution (S/N=3). Moreover, the sensor exhibits fast response time (2s), high sensitivity (74.57μAμmolL−1 cm−2), as well as reproducibility (RSD≤8%), and can maintain the stability at 4°C for two weeks.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene possesses a extended honeycomb network that is consisted of the robust covalent bonds between sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, and represents many intrinsic properties including excellent electron mobility, wide electrochemical potential window, strong mechanical flexibility, high thermal/chemical stability, large specific surface area, and good anti-fouling ability [1]. Therefore, the graphene and its derivatives have been intensively studied in the last decade for their extensive applications in solar cells [2], lithium-ion batteries [3], electronic devices [4], biosensing [5], catalysis [6], biomedical technologies [7] etc.

With desirable structure features, graphene provides an excellent platform for tailoring its surface properties to produce task-specific materials [8]. Many researches have shown that chemical modification should be a powerful strategy to intrinsically regulate the elemental local composition to improve the electronic properties of grapheme [9]. Graphene oxide (GO) produced in the chemical oxidation–exfoliation–reduction process bears carboxyl, carbonyl, peroxide and epoxide groups, and hence its surface structure and properties can be manipulated by means of the moderate reactivity of those oxygen groups to conjugate with various functional components before turned into graphene [10]. Besides chemical modification, the noncovalent modification provides a relatively facile functionalization way to produce the desired materials, and various nanocomposites out of graphene or its derivatives have been prepared by the interactions of van der Waals forces and π−π stacking with polymers, biomolecules, drugs, surfactants, quantum dots, metals or metal oxide nanoparticles, and other nanomaterials [11–13].

Ionic liquids (ILs) are low-temperature molten salts, and their fine-tuned physicochemical properties appeal highly to a wide range of applications from designer “green” solvents, electrolytes, to functional materials [14–16]. ILs can involve in many interactions of Coulombic forces, van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, π−π stacking, electron pair donor/acceptor interactions; and make them present the characteristics to dissolve many inorganic and/or organic compounds in environment-friendly synthesis [17,18]. In addition, the high intrinsic ion conductivity promotes ILs attractive as high-stability materials in emerging high-energy-density and low-cost batteries, supercapacitors, and even sensing materials [19,20]. Tailoring the features of ILs and RG together, therefore, it is expected that graphene/IL nanocomposites would be the excellent materials for practical electrochemical applications [21,22]. However, the coupling of ILs with graphene to take sufficiently advantage of their unique properties has received little attention up to now.

In this work, we focus on the strategy of engineering graphene/quantum dot interfaces with ILs to prepare the nanocomposites of superior electrochemical properties. The surfaces of reduced graphene oxide (RG) were covalently modified by the reaction of GO with 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide prior to reduction. CdTe quantum dots (QDs) capped by sodium mercaptopropionate, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate, and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate were respectively synthesized by the hydrothermal route. The IL functionalized nanocomposites could be constructed by the interface interactions of the resulting RG with CdTe QDs, and exhibit good dispersity and critical improvement in the effective electrode area, saturating absorption capacity, and charge-transfer resistance. Furthermore, the nanocomposites were used as electrochemical sensing materials for the detection of puerarin from the water and human plasma samples. Compared with the results reported in literature, it is shown that the puerarin electrochemical sensor proposed in this work is superior not only to the conventional analytical methods, but also to other electrochemical sensors in sensitivity and linear range of concentration as listed in Table S1.

1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([amim]Br, 99%), 1-ethyl-3- methylimidazolium bromide ([emim]Br, 99%), and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide ([bmim]Br, 99%) were purchased from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Graphite powder (99.95%), Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98%), Anion exchange resin (Ambersep 900 OH), puerarin (≥98%), NaBH4 (98%), Tellurium powder (5N, metals basis), CdCl2 (99.99%, metals basis), KOH (95%), NH3·H2O (≥25%w/w), glucose (≥99.5%), ethylenediamine (EA, 98%), and isopropanol (≥99.5%) were purchased from aladdin reagent com. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without further purification.

1-Ethyl-3-methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate ([emim]MP), and 1-butyl-3- methylimidazolium mercaptopropionate ([bmim]MP) were respectively prepared from [emim]Br and [bmim]Br using the standard anion exchange procedures, and subsequently following an acid–base reaction [23,24]. Typically, anion exchange resin (Ambersep OH−1 form) was fully washed through a great deal of water, and compactly loaded into a glass column of 3cm diameter. Then, aqueous solution of 1wt% [emim]Br or [bmim]Br was added into the resin column to exchange bromine by OH−, and the aqueous solution of [emim][OH] or [bmim][OH] was collected from the effluent. A MPA aqueous solution was added dropwise to the exchanged IL solution with stirring at room temperature up to pH=5.4 as monitoring from pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Co.), after that, the mixture was further stirred for another 12h until this pH holds constant. Finally, water was removed at 40°C by rotatory evaporator, and the resulting product of [emim]MP or [bmim]MP was dried in vacuum at 60°C for 72h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of functional ligand-capped CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs were synthesized according to a similar procedure as reported in our previous work [25]. In a typical run, NaHTe aqueous solution was prepared by the reaction of tellurium with NaBH4. 45.6mg NaBH4 and 38.4mg tellurium power were dispersed in 6mL of N2-saturated deionic water. After the mixture was intensively stirred at 0°C for 6h, a colorless supernatant solution of NaHTe was formed with a white precipitate of sodium tetraborate in the round-bottom flask. For the aqueous phase synthesis of CdTe QDs, 4mL CdCl2 solution of 0.025molL−1 and 1mL [emim]MP solution of 0.0425molL−1 were mixed with 4.5mL EA solution of 0.1molL−1 by stirring at room temperature. After the mixture was purged by bubbling N2 for 30min, freshly prepared 0.5mL NaHTe solution of 0.025molL−1 was added with intensely stirring, yielding the bright-yellow precursor solution of CdTe NCs. Subsequently, the precursor solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a volume of 20mL. The autoclave was heated to and maintained at 80°C for 5h to grow CdTe QDs, and then cooled to room temperature to obtain the red solution of [emim]MP-capped CdTe QDs, which is denoted as CdTe@[emim]MP. After mixed with isopropanol, CdTe@[emim]MP solution was centrifugated at 8000rpm and redispersed in deionized water. The purified process was carried out for three cycles.

The steps employed for the synthesis of CdTe@[bmim]MP or CdTe@MP QDs are same as those mentioned above. However, the dosage of [bmim]MP, NaMP, and NaHTe was a little different in order to incubate the monodisperse QDs. The optimal reaction conditions were that the molar ration of Cd2+:[bmim]MP:HTe− in the precursor mixture was controlled at 5:3.5:1 to synthesize CdTe@[bmim]MP, and was 2:5:1 for the synthesis of CdTe@MP.

GO was prepared from graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [26,27], and the surface functionalization of graphene was carried out prior to the reduction of GO [28–30]. With the aid of ultrasonication for 30min, [amim]Br of 20mg and KOH of 10mg were dispersed in 10mL GO aqueous suspension of 0.5mgmL−1 to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was refluxed at 80°C for 24h. The resulting product, amimGO, was purified by centrifugation at 18,000rpm and redispersed in deionized water repeatedly for three cycles. Then, 80mg of glucose was mixed with 10mL amimGO aqueous suspension of 0.5mgmL−1 at room temperature and stirred vigorously for 30min. After treated further with 40μL NH3·H2O, the mixture was allowed to react at 100°C for 60min. Finally, the ion liquid-modified RG, amimRG, was collected by centrifugation and washed by deionized water repeatedly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of nanocomposite and modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite was fabricated by dispersing amim-RG in the aqueous solution of CdTe@[emim]MP QDs, and then subjected to ultrasonic agitation for 30min to ensure the formation of homogeneous suspension. Then, water was completely evaporated at 40°C, and the resulting nanocomposite was denoted as CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG. For comparison, the nanocomposites of CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG and CdTe@MP-amimRG were respectively prepared by employing the similar way. The CdTe content in these nanocomposite formulations all is 30wt% unless otherwise specified.

The modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was prepared using a standard procedures. GCE was polished with 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05μm alumina slurries in turn, sonicated in deionized water and ethanol, and dried under N2. Then, 6.5μL homogeneous CdTe@[emim]MP-AmimRG nanocomposite suspension was coated on the fresh prepared GCE surface, and dried at the room temperature. The resulting modified electrode was denoted as CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE. The modified electrodes of amimRG/GCE, CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG/GCE, and CdTe@MP- amimRG/GCE were respectively constructed using the similar manner described above.

The sample solution was dropped onto copper grid and dried under vacuum, and the images were taken from JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance using Cu Kα1 (1.54056Å) radiation. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectral data were collected on a Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer. The UV–vis (UV–vis) spectra were measured on a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Raman spectra measurements were conducted on a Horiba Aramis with a 514nm laser light source. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a Netzsch STA449C at the heating rate of 5°Cmin−1 under N2.

Electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Co. Ltd. Shanghai, China) installed a three- electrode cell. A modified GCE (CHI104, d=3mm) was used as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry was conducted from 0.2 to 1.0V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1. The differential pulse voltammetry was performed using H2SO4 aqueous solution of pH=1 as supporting electrolyte with the increment potential of 0.004V, pulse amplitude of 0.05V, pulse width of 0.06s, sample width of 0.0167s, pulse period of 0.2s, and quiet time of 2s. The electrical impedance spectroscopy was measured in 2.5mmolL−1 [(Fe(CN)6]3−/[(Fe(CN)6]4− (1:1) solution containing 0.5molL−1 KCl over a frequency range from 0.1 to 105 Hz and with initiative potential of 0.21V, amplitude of 0.005V, and quiet time of 2s.

CdTe QDs stabilized by [emim]MP were synthesized using the hydrothermal route, and the presence of EA in reaction mixture acts as the critical roles for successfully harvesting the QDs. Not only does the complexation of EA with Cd2+ inhibit Cd(OH)2 from formation, but also the alkaline solution (pH=11) hold by EA is propitious to the coordination of thiol groups in MP ions toward CdTe QDs. In this situations, the counter-ions of [emim]+ distribute around CdTe QDs via electrostatic force, and relatively is more “free” than MP− ions. The TEM image of as-prepared CdTe@[emim]MP QDs represents good uniformity in size with an average diameter of 3.5±0.3nm (Fig. 1 ). The high-resolution TEM image taken from a selected individual nanocrystal gives the clearly resolved lattice spacing of 0.211nm corresponding to the (220) plane of CdTe crystal, and implies the good crystallinity over a single nanoparticle.

The oxygen functional groups impart GO nanosheets the intrinsic characteristics to surface chemical modification. The as-synthesized GO exhibits several typical bands in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 A): the bands at 1042cm−1 and 1195cm−1 are respectively ascribed to the epoxy and alkoxy CO stretching vibration; the bands 1402 and 3363cm−1 respectively to the OH deformation and stretching; the band at 1615cm−1 to aromatic CC stretching; the band at 1717cm−1 to the CO stretching [18,28]. After grafting of the amine-terminated IL from the GO sheets and subsequently reducing process, the disappearance of characteristic GO vibration bands indicates that the frame of sp2 carbon atoms is well restored except the aromatic CC stretching band shifts from 1615 to 1630cm−1. Moreover, it is detected that some new vibration bands come out of the FT-IR spectrum of amimRG. The CN stretching vibration band appears at 1117cm−1, and the CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration bands at 2856 and 2916cm−1 [31]. These FT-IR data confirm the [amim]+ ions are covalently combined on the RG nanosheets.

The expected product of single layer amimRG nanosheet can be further revealed by Raman spectroscopy and XRD measurements. The D and G bands of amimGO in Raman spectrum respectively appear at 1335cm−1 and 1681cm−1 (Fig. 2B). As the amimGO was turned into amimRG, no shift of D band is observed in the Raman spectrum, but the G band shifts from 1681 to 1601cm−1 with a significant decrease in relative intensity, at the same time, the 2D band of amimRG at 2711cm−1 appears as a single symmetric peak. These data suggest that the as-prepared amimRG is the single layer graphene derivative, and its most of sp2 domains are recovered in the reduction process [32,33]. The XRD pattern of GO sheets represents a (001) peak at 2θ=10.7° that corresponds to a d-spacing of 0.8nm due to the presence of oxygenated functional groups (Fig. 2C). The (001) diffraction peak of amimGO is hardly distinguishable, and shows the d-spacing larger than that of GO as the [amim]+ ions anchor covalently on the surfaces and further separate these single layers well. A broad diffraction peak of amimRG nanosheets appears at 2θ=25.3° that corresponds to the typical (002) peak of graphite due to the reduction of amimGO [34,35].

The specially engineering interfaces of amimRG provide the powerful scaffolds for the introduction of QDs, and lead to the enhanced dispersibility and stability of CdTe nanocrystals in the composites. Because of the strong interactions among alkylmethylimidazolium ions common on the interfaces of the two components, CdTe QDs could almost uniformly spread over the amimRG (Fig. S1). The (220) and (311) diffraction peaks in XRD pattern (Fig. 2C) are in agreement with the diffraction rings in the selected-area electron diffraction pattern (Fig. 2S) that could be indexed to the zinc blende structure of bulk CdTe crystal, and reveal the occurrence of CdTe QDs in the nanocomposite although the FT-IR of CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG is similar to that of amimRG (Fig. 2A). Moreover, the constituent contents in the nanocomposite can be roughly evaluated by the data of TGA. There are two temperatures of significant mass loss in the TGA curve of GO (Fig. 2D): one around 183°C results from the decomposition of labile oxygen functional groups, and the other around 563°C is ascribed to the pyrolysis of carbon skeleton[36]. Although the TGA of amimGO displays a less featured curve because of the [amim]Br modification, the mass of IL grafted in RG can be approximately evaluated to be 10.0wt% from the TGA curve. Correspondingly, the [emim]MP content in this nanocomposite can be work out at 16.8%, and the CdTe content at 30.1% (Fig. 2D). It is observed that the complexation of the QDs with amimRG nanosheets causes the rise in thermal decomposition temperatures of [emim]MP ligand and RG carbon skeleton which respectively go up around 269 and 677°C for CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG composite. As shown soon after, it is the high alkylmethylimidazolium content and indwelling synergistic interactions of the [emim]+ ions with RG that makes the nanocomposite exhibits strong adsorption and good electrochemical performances.

The alternating current impedance spectra are well known to reflect the interface properties of electrodes [37]. Employing [(Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution as the redox probe, the charge-transfer resistances (Rct) on the electrode surface were evaluated by the Nyquist plots of the nanocomposite-modified electrodes. As shown in Fig. 3 A and Table 1 , the semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plots decreases from bare GCE, RG/GEC, amimRG/GCE, to CdTe@MP-amimRG/GCE. Contrastively, the Nyquist plots of CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE and CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG/GCE display very small semicircle at high frequencies, and almost are straight lines with a larger slope for CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE than that for CdTe@[bmim]MP- amimRG/GCE. These experimental results indicate that CdTe QDs capped by the ILs can facilitate the electron transfer kinetics in the nanocompomposites, and greatly improve the Rct of the modified electrodes. This could be ascribed to the superior electrical conductivity of alkylmethylimidazolium ions that are relatively “free” in ligand shells of QDs. Although the Na+ counter-ions occur in the ligand shells of CdTe@MP QDs, the Rct of CdTe@MP-amimRG/GCE is more beyond a factor of ten when compared with those of CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE and CdTe@[bmim]MP- amimRG/GCE. Therefore, it is believed that the exceptional conductivity should be a result of the synergistic interactions among the functionalized graphene and the imidazole rings, and presumably the π−π interactions between the graphene surface and the imidazole ring, as well as the small band gap of RG, facilitate the electron transfer.

Puerarin (7,4-dihydroxyisoflavone-8-glucopyranoside) obtained from the kudzu root is a major prescribed drug for patients with cardiocerebrovascular diseases. However, the popular use has caused the increasing reports of adverse drug reactions. In order to develop the efficient electrochemical sensors to monitor the trace active ingredient in sample, the electrochemical performances of nanocomposite-modified GCE were studied by cyclic voltammetry using puerarin as the probe. The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of different electrodes in puerarin aqueous solution were measured under exact same conditions (Fig. 3B). It is observed the bare GCE hardly exhibits electrochemical response toward puerarin, but the ionic liquid-modified graphene/GCE shows the significant improvement in oxidation peak currents (Ipa) and the enhancement in the sensitivity. The CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE represents the best sensing performance, along with the low charge-transfer resistance, and hence is selected the preference electrode for the electrochemical sensor of puerarin.

In order to revealing the possible electrode reaction mechanism, the CVs of CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE in a given puerarin aqueous solution were measured at different scan rates. It is shown that the change of Ipa with scan rate (υ) represents a linear relationship from 50 to 300mVs−1, and can be expressed as Ipa (μA)=−0.180υ (mVs−1) −28.418 (R2 =0.9999) by curve fitting (Fig. 3C). This experimental fact suggests that the electrode reaction is mainly adsorption-controlled in the selected scan rate range. [38] With the intent of building electrochemical sensors for puerarin, therefore, it is necessary to determine several important adsorption parameters of the nanocomposites, including the physical adsorption, electrode electroactive area, and saturating absorption capacity etc.

Puerarin has two absorption bands at 249 and 305nm, and the physical adsorptive characteristics of CdTe@[emim]MP-AmimRG could be demonstrated by the absorbance of puerarin aqueous solutions. After the nanocomposite suspension was equilibrated with puerarin aqueous solution of 50μmolL−1 at room temperature for 15min, the absorption spectrum of puerarin was collected from the resulting supernatant solution (Fig. 30 D). The adsorptive capacity (Γ in Table 1) can be determined by the standard work curve: the dependence of absorbance at 249nm on puerarin concentration (Aabs =0.0229c (μmolL−1) −0.2021). Obviously, the Γ is related to the interactions of puerarin with the counter ions in ligand shells of QDs, and the presence of ILs is advantage to the physical adsorption of nanocomposites. Each gram of CdTe@[emim]MP-AmimRG nanocomposite is able to absorb 13.3mg of puerarin from the aqueous solution, and this highest adsorptive capacity results possibly from the strongest hydrogen bonding that [emim]+ ions have.

The chronocoulometry was used to evaluate electrochemically the effective electrode area (A) and the saturating absorption capacity (Γ*). The chronocoulometric curves of the bare GCE was measured in 1mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1molL−1 KCl, and the linear relation of Q vs. t1/2 could be obtained by fitting (Fig. 4 A and B). According to the Anson equation [39],
(1)
Q =
2 n F A c D
1 / 2
t
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
+
Q
d l
+
Q
a d s

In which c is the concentration of active ingredient, D is the diffusion coefficient of active ingredient, t is the potential pulse width, Qdl is the double-layer charge, Qads is the Faradaic charge of adsorbed active ingredient. It has been known that c=1.0mmolL−1, n=1, D=7.6×10−6 cm2 s−1 for the K3[Fe(CN)6] solution [40], and the electrochemically effective electrode area of the bare GCE was calculated to be 0.056cm−2 from the slope of the straight line of Q∼t1/2. Using the same procedures, the effective electrode areas of the nanocomposite-functionalized GCE were respectively measured, and they all represent the larger effective surface area than that of the bare GCE (Table 1). Among them, the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE displays the maximal electrode area that almost is 8 times more than that of the bare GCE.

Taking the calculated effective electrode areas into consideration, the saturating absorption capacities of puerarin on the electrode surfaces could be determined by the chronocoulometry. In the absence and presence of 5mmolL−1 puerarin, the chronocoulometric curves of the different electrodes were respectively recorded in aqueous H2SO4 solution of pH=1, and the corresponding correlations of Q∼t1/2 were obtained by linear fitting (Fig. 4C and D). Following the Laviron’s theory:
(2)
Q
a d s
=
n F A T
*

The saturating absorption capacities can be respectively calculated from the corresponding intercepts of linear regression, and follow the order: Γ* (bare GCE)<Γ* (RG/GCE)<Γ* (amimRG/GCE)<Γ* (CdTe@MP-amimRG/GCE)<Γ* (CdTe@[bmim]MP- amimRG/GCE)<Γ* (CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE) (Table 1). The order is well in line with that of the effective electrode area, and also with that of the Qdl as shown from the intercepts in Fig. 4C, suggesting the presence of QDs enlarges the electrochemical active surface area and the loading amount of puerarin on the nanocomposite electrodes. Significantly, the Γ* depends on the properties of counter-ions in the ligand shells of QDs. The Na+ ions have small diameter and weak interactions with puerarin and [amim]+ ions grafted on the RG, hence, the CdTe@MP-amimRG composite displays small A and Γ*. However, the alkylmethylimidazolium ions in the nanocomposites better improve the A and Γ* due to their larger diameters and stronger interactions with puerarin and amimRG. It is noted that [emim]+ ions exert more positive roles on the Γ* of nanocomposites than [bmim]+ ions do, implying that the enhanced hydrophobicity of the ILs would impair the Γ* although the long alkyl chains enlarge the volume of the alkylmethylimidazolium ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing for the detection of puerarin
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite-functionalized GCE exhibit the large A, great Γ*, low Rct, as well as wide electrochemical potential window comparable to that of the primitive GCE, as a result, it is expected that the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG could act as the attractive electrochemical sensing composites. Because the redox process of puerarin is adsorption-controlled on CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE, the differential pulse voltammetry that has higher current sensitivity and better resolution for the adsorbate could be used to evaluate the as-prepared composite as the electrochemical sensor [41]. After the accumulation potential of 0.6V was hold constant for 250s, the differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) on CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE were collected from the different concentration puerarin aqueous solutions with the optimizing pH (Fig. S3). It is observed that the oxidation peak current (Ipa) decreases with the puerarin concentration (c), and follows a linear relation of Ipa (μA)=–5.268c (μmolL−1) −0.930 (R=0.9999) in the concentration range of 4–0.01μmolL−1 with the sensitivity of 74.57μAμmolL−1 cm−2 (Fig. 5 A and Table 2 ). Based on the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, the limit of detection (L.O.D.) was determined to be 6nmolL−1.

In addition to the high sensitivity and reproducibility as summarized in Tables 2 and 3, the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG/GCE displays the good stability and anti-interferences. Five nanocomposite electrodes were respectively built by employing the same process, and the relative standard deviation was 1.91% for the current response of these electrodes in CVs of 10μmolL−1 puerarin solution containing H2SO4 (pH=1). After these electrodes were kept at 4°C for two weeks, the oxidation peak current attenuates 4.8% of the original current. The interference test of potential presence ions and compounds in real samples was performed by cyclic voltammetry using the aqueous puerarin solution of 10μmolL−1. Under the same experimental conditions, no interferences (relative error less than 4, Table S2) were detected as the 800 times of Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Cl−, and 50 times of ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, amino acids (glycine, proline, DL-alanine) more than the puerarin concentration were respectively added into the sample.

CdTe QDs were synthesized respectively using NaMP, [emim]MP, and [bmim]MP as the stabilizers. These surface ligand-functionalized QDs are prone to combine with the amine-terminated [amim]Br grafted at the surfaces of the single layer reduced graphene by the interface interactions, and form the well dispersed QDs-RG nanocomposites with high IL content. Further, the properties of physical adsorptive capacity, effective electrode area, saturating absorption capacity and charge-transfer resistance were systemically determined from bare GCE, amimRG, CdTe@MP-amimRG, CdTe@[bmim]MP-amimRG, and CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG. The results show that the large and hydrophilic alkylmethylimidazolium ions have strong synergistic interactions with the single layer graphene, and are responsible to the significant improvement in nanocomposite performances. When the CdTe@[emim]MP-amimRG composite was used to modify the GCE, the resulting electrochemical sensor exhibits high sensitivity, precision, and stability in detection of puerarin from water and human plasma, indicating the feasibility of the nanocomposite-modified electrode in environmental or biological analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21073055 and 21573059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.11.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hucheng Zhang received his PhD degree in physical chemistry in 2004 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Now he is a full professor in Henan Normal University. His research is focused on the green synthesis for functional materials and applications.
Yingying Shang is a Master degree candidate in Henan Normal University. Her current research interest is focused on electrochemical sensing and electrocatalysis properties of functionalized graphene.
Ting Zhang received her Master degree candidate in Henan Normal University. Her current research interest is focused on novel nanomaterials −based sensors and biosensors.
Kelei Zhuo is a full professor in Henan Normal University, His current research work concentrates on the application of nanostructured-materials in electrochemistry.
Jianji Wang is a full professor in Henan Normal University, and His research involves in green chemistry.
